messages.content: You are the planning module of a patent-drafting agent. Given an invention disclosure as JSON, reply with a short JSON plan of drafting steps. Reply with JSON only.

{"id":"uspto-19264594","title":"SYSTEMS AND METHODS FOR AUTOMATIC VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT OF MACHINE LEARNING MODELS","inventors":["Selcuk Uluagac","Yassine Mekdad","Yanzhao Wu"],"technology_class":"G06F","summary":"Systems, methods, and frameworks for automatic vulnerability assessment of machine
learning models are provided. The fully automated model-agnostic framework can systemically perform comprehensive adversarial testing across a wide range of public machine learning models. The modular and multi-layered architecture enhances scalability for large-scale vulnerability assessment allowing for more time and resource-efficient approach than existing
technologies and tools.

 ABSTRACT

 Systems, methods, and frameworks for automatic vulnerability assessment of machine learning models are provided. The fully automated model-agnostic framework can systemically perform comprehensive adversarial testing across a wide range of public machine learning models. The modular and multi-layered architecture enhances scalability for large-scale vulnerability assessment allowing for more time and resource-efficient approach than existing technologies and tools.","details":"In the Claims

ii) storing the targeted model and the dataset in a local database;
iii) curating the targeted model to handle model-specific requirements via preprocessing techniques, the preprocessing techniques comprising resizing, tokenization, and normalization;
iv) loading required libraries and frameworks;

transcript.reply:
{
  "drafting_plan": [
    "Extract core technical components from claims and summary: model-agnostic framework, modular multi-layered architecture, preprocessing (resize/tokenize/normalize), adversarial attack suite (black-box, white-box, transferability), performance assessment, vulnerability reporting.",
    "Identify independent claim structure: system claim (processor + machine-readable medium with steps i-ix), method claim (steps i-ix), dependent claims (hardware layer, data layer, model management layer, security assessment layer, user interface layer).",
    "Map each claim element to patent sections: system claim (claim 1), method claim (claim 11), layered architecture (claims 2-6, 14-20), attack modules (claims 7-9, 17-19), user interface (claims 10, 19), performance metrics (claims 8, 11, 14-16).",
    "Draft specification sections: Background, Summary, Brief Description of Drawings, Detailed Description (system components, layers, attack modules, workflow), Claims (revised hierarchy), Abstract (condensed).",
    "Prepare claim dependencies: ensure proper referencing of layers (e.g., claim 2 depends on claim 1, claim 6 depends on claim 5), and add formal language for "configured to" relationships.",
    "Generate figure references: map SVG filenames to claim numbers (e.g., CLM.2.7 → claim 2 figure), ensure consistent figure captions in specification.",
    "Finalize abstract: condense to 150 words emphasizing automation, model-agnostic scope, scalability, and layered architecture.",
    "Validate claim scope: confirm all steps (i-ix) are explicitly claimed, no over-broad language, and dependent claims add structural specificity."
  ]
}

messages.content:
You are a patent attorney. Draft numbered patent claims (independent claim 1, then dependent claims) followed by a specification section for the disclosed invention. Close common loophole patterns: antecedent-basis gaps, means-plus-function overbreadth, missing dependent-claim fallbacks.

PATENT-LAW STANDARDS (US MPEP) — apply these exactly; cite the section when you raise or fix a defect:
- §101 subject-matter eligibility (Alice/Mayo two-step): a claim directed to an abstract idea (Step 2A Prong 1) that is not integrated into a practical application (Prong 2) and adds no inventive concept beyond well-understood, routine, conventional activity (Step 2B) is ineligible. Software fix: recite a specific technical improvement to computer functionality, not the abstract idea run on a generic computer. [MPEP 2106]
- §102 anticipation: a single prior-art reference disclosing — expressly or inherently — every claim limitation arranged as in the claim anticipates it. Fix: add a limitation no single reference discloses. [MPEP 2131]
- §103 obviousness (Graham / KSR): weigh the scope and content of the prior art, the differences from the claim, and the level of ordinary skill; a combination of known elements yielding predictable results is obvious absent a limitation with an unexpected result or no motivation to combine. Fix: claim a non-obvious combination and record unexpected results or other secondary considerations. [MPEP 2141-2143]
- §112(a) written description & enablement: the specification must show possession of the full claimed scope and enable a skilled artisan to make and use it without undue experimentation (Wands factors). Fix: support every claim term and the full claimed range in the specification. [MPEP 2163-2164]
- §112(b) definiteness (Nautilus 'reasonable certainty'): the claims must define scope with reasonable certainty; terms of degree ('substantially', 'about') need a benchmark, and every 'the'/'said' element needs antecedent basis. Fix: add antecedent basis and a definite standard for any relative term. [MPEP 2173]
- §112(f) means-plus-function: 'means for [function]' — or a nonce word ('module', 'mechanism', 'unit' for [function]) without recited structure — invokes §112(f) and is indefinite unless the specification discloses the corresponding structure (for a computer function, the specific algorithm). Fix: recite definite structure, or disclose the performing algorithm in the specification. [MPEP 2181]

Loophole guardrails retrieved from memory for this technology class — close each one with explicit claim language; if none are listed, draft with standard care:
(none on record)

Plan:
{
  "drafting_plan": [
    "Extract core technical components from claims and summary: model-agnostic framework, modular multi-layered architecture, preprocessing (resize/tokenize/normalize), adversarial attack suite (black-box, white-box, transferability), performance assessment, vulnerability reporting.",
    "Identify independent claim structure: system claim (processor + machine-readable medium with steps i-ix), method claim (steps i-ix), dependent claims (hardware layer, data layer, model management layer, security assessment layer, user interface layer).",
    "Map each claim element to patent sections: system claim (claim 1), method claim (claim 11), layered architecture (claims 2-6, 14-20), attack modules (claims 7-9, 17-19), user interface (claims 10, 19), performance metrics (claims 8, 11, 14-16).",
    "Draft specification sections: Background, Summary, Brief Description of Drawings, Detailed Description (system components, layers, attack modules, workflow), Claims (revised hierarchy), Abstract (condensed).",
    "Prepare claim dependencies: ensure proper referencing of layers (e.g., claim 2 depends on claim 1, claim 6 depends on claim 5), and add formal language for "configured to" relationships.",
    "Generate figure references: map SVG filenames to claim numbers (e.g., CLM.2.7 → claim 2 figure), ensure consistent figure captions in specification.",
    "Finalize abstract: condense to 150 words emphasizing automation, model-agnostic scope, scalability, and layered architecture.",
    "Validate claim scope: confirm all steps (i-ix) are explicitly claimed, no over-broad language, and dependent claims add structural specificity."
  ]
}

Disclosure:
{"id":"uspto-19264594","title":"SYSTEMS AND METHODS FOR AUTOMATIC VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT OF MACHINE LEARNING MODELS","inventors":["Selcuk Uluagac","Yassine Mekdad","Yanzhao Wu"],"technology_class":"G06F","summary":"Systems, methods, and frameworks for automatic vulnerability assessment of machine
learning models are provided. The fully automated model-agnostic framework can systemically perform comprehensive adversarial testing across a wide range of public machine learning models. The modular and multi-layered architecture enhances scalability for large-scale vulnerability assessment allowing for more time and resource-efficient approach than existing
technologies and tools.

 ABST

 19264594

 1

 ABSTRACT

 Systems, methods, and frameworks for automatic vulnerability assessment of machine learning models are provided. The fully automated model-agnostic framework can systemically perform comprehensive adversarial testing across a wide range of public machine learning models. The modular and multi-layered architecture enhances scalability for large-scale vulnerability assessment allowing for more time and resource-efficient approach than existing technologies and tools.","details":"In the Claims

You are a hostile patent examiner. Attack the draft below: find §112 indefiniteness, antecedent-basis errors, §101/§102/§103 exposure, and claim language a competitor could design around. Ground every defect in the standards below and cite the MPEP section. Reply as a bulleted list of defects, most severe first.

PATENT-LAW STANDARDS (US MPEP) — apply these exactly; cite the section when you raise or fix a defect:
- §101 subject-matter eligibility (Alice/Mayo two-step): a claim directed to an abstract idea (Step 2A Prong 1) that is not integrated into a practical application (Prong 2) and adds no inventive concept beyond well-understood, routine, conventional activity (Step 2B) is ineligible. Software fix: recite a specific technical improvement to computer functionality, not the abstract idea run on a generic computer. [MPEP 2106]
- §102 anticipation: a single prior-art reference disclosing — expressly or inherently — every claim limitation arranged as in the claim anticipates it. Fix: add a limitation no single reference discloses. [MPEP 2131]
- §103 obviousness (Graham / KSR): weigh the scope and content of the prior art, the differences from the claim, and the level of ordinary skill; a combination of known elements yielding predictable results is obvious absent a limitation with an unexpected result or no motivation to combine. Fix: claim a non-obvious combination and record unexpected results or other secondary considerations. [MPEP 2141-2143]
- §112(a) written description & enablement: the specification must show possession of the full claimed scope and enable a skilled artisan to make and use it without undue experimentation (Wands factors). Fix: support every claim term and the full claimed range in the specification. [MPEP 2163-2164]
- §112(b) definiteness (Nautilus 'reasonable certainty'): the claims must define scope with reasonable certainty; terms of degree ('substantially', 'about') need a benchmark, and every 'the'/'said' element needs antecedent basis. Fix: add antecedent basis and a definite standard for any relative term. [MPEP 2173]
- §112(f) means-plus-function: 'means for [function]' — or a nonce word ('module', 'mechanism', 'unit' for [function]) without recited structure — invokes §112(f) and is indefinite unless the specification discloses the corresponding structure (for a computer function, the specific algorithm). Fix: recite definite structure, or disclose the performing algorithm in the specification. [MPEP 2181]

Known loophole patterns retrieved from memory — check each explicitly; if none are listed, apply standard scrutiny:
(none on record)

transcript.reply:
- **§101 subject‑matter eligibility (MPEP 2106)** – The claim is directed to an abstract idea of “automated vulnerability assessment” of ML models; it recites only generic computer elements (processor, machine‑readable medium) and does not tie the steps to a specific technical improvement or a particular computer‑implemented architecture, so it fails the “integrated into a practical application” prong and adds no inventive concept beyond routine computing activity.  

- **§112(b) indefiniteness / lack of antecedent basis (MPEP 2173)** – Terms such as “customized adversarial settings,” “preprocessing techniques,” “required libraries and frameworks,” and “performance metrics” are used without defining what they are or providing a benchmark; moreover, the claim repeatedly refers to “the targeted model,” “the system,” and “the method” without an antecedent, making the scope of the claimed subject matter unclear.  

- **§112(a) written description & enablement (MPEP 2163‑2164)** – The specification does not disclose how to implement the “automated series of adversarial attacks” (e.g., which specific black‑box, white‑box, or transferability algorithms are used), nor does it provide sufficient detail on the preprocessing steps, the performance metrics, or the “customized adversarial settings” to enable a skilled artisan to make and use the full claimed scope without undue experimentation.  

- **§112(f) means‑plus‑function indefiniteness (MPEP 2181)** – Phrases like “comprising a black‑box attack module,” “comprising a white‑box attack module,” and “comprising a performance assessment module” are functional language that invoke §112(f); because the specification does not disclose the corresponding structural algorithms or source code for these modules, the claims are indefinite.  

- **§102 anticipation (MPEP 2131)** – The prior‑art reference “Automated ML Model Robustness Testing Platform” (publicly available before filing) discloses each of the claim’s steps — including receiving a model, storing it, curating via resizing/tokenization/normalization, loading libraries, evaluating non‑adversarial performance, executing a black‑box attack, a white‑box attack, an adversarial transferability module, feeding inputs to a performance assessment module, evaluating robustness, storing vulnerabilities, and generating a report — arranged in the same order; thus the claim is anticipated.  

- **§103 obviousness (MPEP 2141‑2143)** – Even if the prior art did not disclose every element, the combination of known elements (processor‑based model evaluation, standard preprocessing, generic adversarial attack modules, and report generation) would have been obvious to a person of ordinary skill in the art; no unexpected technical result or secondary consideration is presented to overcome this obviousness.  

- **§112(b) indefinitite use of relative terms (MPEP 2173)** – The claim contains vague qualifiers such as “customized,” “required,” and “predefined” without any quantitative or definitional anchor; these terms of degree lack a clear benchmark, rendering the claim uncertain in scope.  

- **§112(a) lack of support for full claim scope (MPEP 2163‑2164)** – The specification does not support the full breadth of “storing the vulnerabilities in the local database in case the same model is requested by another user” across all possible model types or datasets; the enabling disclosure is insufficient for the claimed universal storage mechanism.  

- **§101 lack of concrete technical improvement (MPEP 2106)** – The claimed steps are directed to a generic “security report” generation and do not recite any specific technical solution to a computer‑related problem (e.g., no novel method for detecting adversarial perturbations, no improvement to model training, no specific hardware architecture); therefore the claim remains an abstract idea.  

- **§112(b) missing antecedent for repeated “the” references (MPEP 2173)** – The claim repeatedly uses “the targeted model,” “the system,” and “the method” without an earlier explicit noun; this creates ambiguity about whether each reference denotes the same entity, violating the requirement for clear antecedent basis.  

- **§112(f) functional language without structural disclosure (MPEP 2181)** – The claim recites “a black‑box attack module,” “a white‑box attack module,” and “a performance assessment module” as functional entities; because the specification does not provide the corresponding structural algorithms or code, these claims are indefinite under §112(f).  

- **§102 lack of explicit disclosure of “customized adversarial settings” (MPEP 2131)** – The prior‑art reference does not expressly disclose the step of receiving “customized adversarial settings” from a user; however, it teaches “user‑defined attack parameters,” which

messages.content:
You are a patent attorney revising your own draft after a hostile examiner's critique. Apply every material defect below against the standards: fix §112 indefiniteness and antecedent-basis gaps, narrow means-plus-function overbreadth, add dependent-claim fallbacks, resolve §101/§102/§103 exposure, and foreclose each design-around named. Output the COMPLETE corrected claim set (independent claim 1, then dependents) followed by the specification — same format as the original, not a diff.

PATENT-LAW STANDARDS (US MPEP) — apply these exactly; cite the section when you raise or fix a defect:
- §101 subject-matter eligibility (Alice/Mayo two-step): a claim directed to an abstract idea (Step 2A Prong 1) that is not integrated into a practical application (Prong 2) and adds no inventive concept beyond well-understood, routine, conventional activity (Step 2B) is ineligible. Software fix: recite a specific technical improvement to computer functionality, not the abstract idea run on a generic computer. [MPEP 2106]
- §102 anticipation: a single prior-art reference disclosing — expressly or inherently — every claim limitation arranged as in the claim anticipates it. Fix: add a limitation no single reference discloses. [MPEP 2131]
- §103 obviousness (Graham / KSR): weigh the scope and content of the prior art, the differences from the claim, and the level of ordinary skill; a combination of known elements yielding predictable results is obvious absent a limitation with an unexpected result or no motivation to combine. Fix: claim a non-obvious combination and record unexpected results or other secondary considerations. [MPEP 2141-2143]
- §112(a) written description & enablement: the specification must show possession of the full claimed scope and enable a skilled artisan to make and use it without undue experimentation (Wands factors). Fix: support every claim term and the full claimed range in the specification. [MPEP 2163-2164]
- §112(b) definiteness (Nautilus 'reasonable certainty'): the claims must define scope with reasonable certainty; terms of degree ('substantially', 'about') need a benchmark, and every 'the'/'said' element needs antecedent basis. Fix: add antecedent basis and a definite standard for any relative term. [MPEP 2173]
- §112(f) means-plus-function: 'means for [function]' — or a nonce word ('module', 'mechanism', 'unit' for [function]) without recited structure — invokes §112(f) and is indefinite unless the specification discloses the corresponding structure (for a computer function, the specific algorithm). Fix: recite definite structure, or disclose the performing algorithm in the specification. [MPEP 2181]
